Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/eidklient-native.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-m_zsp5es...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-m_zsp5es
Analyzing eidklient-native AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-m_zsp5es) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD sources only standard local files (`eidklient`, `patch-qt`, `qt.hook`, `qt6.conf`) and dynamically constructs two `source` URLs for i686 and x86_64 using `printf` and a predefined `_url` template. The dynamic construction uses `pkgver` and `CARCH` to generate an archived HTTPS URL via web.archive.org, which is a trusted archival service. The `printf` command is used solely for string formatting to resolve the final source URL—no command substitution, eval, or code execution results from it. The `sha256sums` are provided for the AppImage tarballs on both architectures, and while the local files have 'SKIP' checksums (normal for AUR packages), no remote execution occurs during sourcing.

The top-level code and `pkgver()` function (which is not defined, so not executed) pose no risk. There is no network interaction or data exfiltration during sourcing. The use of `printf` in `source_i686` and `source_x86_64` is syntactically safe and deterministic, relying only on static format strings and environment variables like `CARCH`. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare --printsrcinfo` will safely fetch and extract the sources without executing any build or prepare steps, and no malicious behavior is possible during this phase.
</details>
<summary>
No remote code execution during source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No remote code execution during source fetch[/]

[0/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD, qt6.conf...
[0/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD, qt6.conf, eidklient.install...
LLM auditresponse for eidklient.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `eidklient.install` script performs standard pre- and post-installation operations consistent with Arch Linux package management practices. It removes files from `/opt/eidklient/lib/patched/` and Qt-related libraries during pre-remove and pre-upgrade stages, which is typical when managing patched or overwritten application libraries. The `post_install` and `post_upgrade` functions call `/opt/eidklient/patch-qt`, a script likely responsible for applying necessary patches to bundled Qt libraries — a documented and expected step for this application, given its name and functionality.

The `checkpcscd` function verifies whether the `pcscd.socket` or `pcscd.service` is enabled and prints a helpful message instructing the user to enable `pcscd.socket` if neither is active. This is a legitimate requirement, as pcscd is necessary for smart card communication, which is essential for an eID client. There are no network operations, obfuscated commands, unauthorized data exfiltration, or execution of unsanctioned code. All operations are confined to expected paths and serve the application’s core functionality.

While the script manipulates files in `/opt/eidklient/` and `/usr/lib/`, this is consistent with the package's purpose and does not extend to unrelated system files or user data. The behavior is transparent and aligns with upstream application needs. No malicious or suspicious activity is present.
</details>
<summary>
Benign installation script with standard operations
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD, qt6.conf...
+ Reviewed eidklient.install. Status: SAFE -- Benign installation script with standard operations
LLM auditresponse for qt6.conf:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `qt6.conf` is a simple configuration file used by Qt6 applications to define runtime paths. It contains a single directive, `Prefix = /usr/lib/qt6`, which sets the installation prefix for Qt6 libraries and binaries. This is a standard and expected configuration for Qt6 on Arch Linux systems, where Qt6 components are typically installed under `/usr/lib/qt6`.

There are no executable commands, network operations, file manipulations, or any form of code in this file. It does not perform downloads, execute external programs, or access sensitive data. The content is purely declarative and consistent with normal Qt6 deployment practices.

Such `.conf` files are commonly used by Qt's `qt.conf` mechanism to override default search paths, especially in packaging contexts where the build and runtime environments are controlled. No obfuscation, suspicious URLs, or dangerous constructs are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Qt6 configuration file with no malicious content.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed qt6.conf. Status: SAFE -- Standard Qt6 configuration file with no malicious content.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a source tarball from web.archive.org, which contains an AppImage for the Slovak eID Client. The archive URL uses a timestamped snapshot (20260203082204 and 20260203082146), consistent with Wayback Machine archival patterns. The use of `printf` to construct these URLs is not obfuscated and matches the predictable structure of archive.org URLs.

The package extracts the AppImage, reorganizes parts of it (splitting main app files from unused libraries), applies necessary local patches for Qt6 compatibility, and installs wrappers and symlinks. It correctly sets up licenses, icons, and desktop files. The dependencies declared are legitimate and security-relevant (e.g. `botan2`, `qt6-base`, `libpcsclite.so`). The build process does not execute arbitrary remote code, exfiltrate data, or modify unrelated system components.

The use of `SKIP` for local sources is expected because those files (eidklient, patch-qt, qt.hook, qt6.conf) are maintained in the AUR package repository itself and are not downloaded from external network sources during build. The actual AppImage-hosting URL is archived and checksummed—both i686 and x86_64 variants have matching, valid sha256sums. No suspicious network calls, obfuscated payloads, or dynamic code downloads are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR packaging with archived, checksummed sources
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for uninstall.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `uninstall.sh` script is designed to remove an AppImage-based installation of the eID_klient application. It locates AppImage files under `/usr/lib/eID_klient` (or a user-specified path), allows the user to select one if multiple are present, extracts an `Uninstall.sh` script from within the AppImage using the `--appimage-extract` flag, makes it executable, runs it with appropriate arguments, and finally removes the extracted files. This behavior is consistent with standard AppImage uninstallation practices, where embedded helper scripts are used for clean removal.

There are no signs of malicious activity such as unauthorized network access, data exfiltration, obfuscated code, or execution of externally fetched content. The use of `sudo` is limited to removing system-level files, which is expected for uninstalling globally installed software. The script operates only on pre-installed components within trusted paths and does not introduce external payloads.

All operations are transparent and focused on uninstallation. No supply-chain risks or security violations are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AppImage uninstall script, no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/6] Reviewing eidklient, patch-qt, qt.hook, src/install.sh, src/updater.sh...
+ Reviewed src/uninstall.sh. Status: SAFE -- Standard AppImage uninstall script, no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for qt.hook:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The qt.hook file defines a Pacman hook that automatically executes a script located at `/opt/eidklient/patch-qt` after installing or upgrading the `qt6-base` package. The `Exec` directive runs the script without any restrictions or verification, and the script's source and functionality are not visible in this file.

Executing untrusted scripts from non-standard paths like `/opt/eidklient/` as part of a system package transaction poses a significant supply-chain risk. If the script were malicious, it could patch Qt libraries in a way that introduces backdoors, intercepts GUI data, or escalates privileges—especially since Qt runs in many user contexts. The lack of code review, origin verification, or checksum validation for the `patch-qt` script makes this a potential vector for code injection.

Although post-transaction hooks are a legitimate packaging mechanism, automatically running an external script outside of the package's build context and in a non-standard directory is suspicious and inconsistent with standard AUR practices.
</details>
<summary>Auto-executes untrusted script post-install</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/6] Reviewing eidklient, patch-qt, src/install.sh, src/updater.sh...
x Reviewed qt.hook. Status: UNSAFE -- Auto-executes untrusted script post-install
LLM auditresponse for install.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The script is unsafe because it executes an AppImage file without verifying its origin or integrity, and proceeds to run an extracted script (`Install.sh`) as root using `sudo`. The line `$FILE $PATTERN $APPIMAGE /usr $VERBOSE` runs the extracted script with elevated privileges and passes user-controllable inputs, including the AppImage path and a regex pattern. Since the script does not validate the contents of the AppImage or the extracted file, an attacker could supply a malicious AppImage that drops harmful code, leading to arbitrary code execution as root.

Furthermore, the script combines user input (via `read -p`) with filesystem operations and privilege escalation without input sanitization or source verification. The fact that it automatically extracts and runs code from an AppImage — a format designed to bundle and execute applications — means that this script effectively allows untrusted code to be silently elevated to root privileges. This behavior constitutes a supply-chain risk and could be exploited to install backdoors or exfiltrate system data.

Even if the AppImage is intended to be from a trusted upstream, the lack of checksum verification, code signing, or sandboxing during installation makes this script dangerously permissive. Such behavior falls outside standard AUR packaging practices and poses a genuine security threat.
</details>
<summary>
Arbitrary root code execution from unverified AppImage</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/6] Reviewing eidklient, patch-qt, src/updater.sh...
x Reviewed src/install.sh. Status: UNSAFE -- Arbitrary root code execution from unverified AppImage
LLM auditresponse for updater.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `updater.sh` performs a dangerous operation by unconditionally removing the entire package directory with `rm -rf "$PACKAGE_DIR"` after attempting to run `install.sh`, regardless of whether that directory contains only temporary files or the original cloned repository. The variable `PACKAGE_DIR` is derived from the script's own location via `$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)`, meaning it points to the directory where this script resides. After the `install.sh` execution, the script changes to `/tmp` and deletes the previously determined `PACKAGE_DIR` — effectively allowing arbitrary deletion of files in the original script's location, which could include user data or other important files if symlinked or misused.

More critically, the script invokes `pkexec bash -c "$PACKAGE_DIR/install.sh"` with elevated privileges. This is equivalent to running an arbitrary script as root without any integrity checks or sandboxing. Combined with the final `rm -rf` on the script's own directory, this creates a path for privilege escalation and persistent system modification: if an attacker replaces or injects code into `install.sh` (or `updater.sh` itself), the script will execute it as root and then attempt to delete the evidence from disk — a behavior typical of malicious updater logic.

This pattern — elevating privileges to run a local script, then deleting the script's source directory — deviates significantly from standard AUR packaging practices. It introduces supply-chain risks and acts as a potential self-deleting backdoor mechanism, especially since there is no verification of the `install.sh` content before execution.
</details>
<summary>
Privilege escalation followed by forced directory deletion</summary>
</security_assessment>

[4/6] Reviewing eidklient, patch-qt...
x Reviewed src/updater.sh. Status: UNSAFE -- Privilege escalation followed by forced directory deletion
LLM auditresponse for patch-qt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The script performs unauthorized file modifications on system libraries (`/usr/lib/libQt6Core.so.6` and `/usr/lib/libQt6Widgets.so.6`) by copying them to a different directory (`/usr/lib/eID_klient/`) and removing a section (`.note.gnu.property`) using `objcopy`. This behavior is highly suspicious and dangerous for several reasons:

First, the script operates on files outside the package build directory, directly accessing `/usr/lib/`, which violates standard packaging practices. Package builds must not modify or depend on the host system’s installed files, especially not by copying binaries from system directories during package installation or patching.

Second, manipulating binary sections of shared libraries can alter runtime behavior, potentially bypassing security mechanisms (e.g., control flow integrity checks). The removal of `.note.gnu.property`, a section used by modern glibc for feature testing and hardening, could weaken security assumptions and is not justified by typical packaging requirements. The use of `objcopy` in this context resembles binary patching often seen in supply chain attacks to disable security checks or inject backdoors.

The script provides no validation that source libraries exist, runs with no safeguards, and assumes absolute system paths, further indicating it is not intended for reproducible, safe packaging.
</details>
<summary>
Modifies system libraries outside build scope</summary>
</security_assessment>

[5/6] Reviewing eidklient...
x Reviewed patch-qt. Status: UNSAFE -- Modifies system libraries outside build scope
LLM auditresponse for eidklient:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided script is a simple launcher script for the `eidklient-native` application. It sets the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to include `/usr/lib/eID_klient` and `/usr/lib`, ensuring that the application can locate its required shared libraries. It then executes the corresponding binary under `/opt/eidklient/` using the same filename as the script itself, passing along all command-line arguments.

The operations performed are consistent with standard packaging practices for binary applications distributed via AUR. Setting `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is a common workaround when binaries are not linked with standard library search paths. The script does not perform any network operations, file exfiltration, dynamic code evaluation (e.g., `eval`, `base64` decoding), or execution of fetched remote content. It only executes a local binary in a predictable location (`/opt/eidklient/`), using a filename derived safely from `$0`.

There is no obfuscation, suspicious downloads, or unauthorized system modifications. The use of `exec` to replace the shell process with the target binary is a normal and secure practice. While modifying `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` can be a security risk in some contexts (e.g., if paths are writable by untrusted users), here the paths are fixed and system-owned. This script poses no supply-chain threat.
</details>
<summary>
Standard launcher script; no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[6/6] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed eidklient. Status: SAFE -- Standard launcher script; no malicious behavior
Reviewed all additional files.
Reviewed additional files.
Audit complete! Result: Unsafe -- DO NOT INSTALL!
# Issues (4 total)

## qt.hook

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Auto-executes untrusted script post-install

Details:

The qt.hook file defines a Pacman hook that automatically executes a script located at `/opt/eidklient/patch-qt` after installing or upgrading the `qt6-base` package. The `Exec` directive runs the script without any restrictions or verification, and the script's source and functionality are not visible in this file.

Executing untrusted scripts from non-standard paths like `/opt/eidklient/` as part of a system package transaction poses a significant supply-chain risk. If the script were malicious, it could patch Qt libraries in a way that introduces backdoors, intercepts GUI data, or escalates privileges—especially since Qt runs in many user contexts. The lack of code review, origin verification, or checksum validation for the `patch-qt` script makes this a potential vector for code injection.

Although post-transaction hooks are a legitimate packaging mechanism, automatically running an external script outside of the package's build context and in a non-standard directory is suspicious and inconsistent with standard AUR practices.

---

## install.sh

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Arbitrary root code execution from unverified AppImage

Details:

The script is unsafe because it executes an AppImage file without verifying its origin or integrity, and proceeds to run an extracted script (`Install.sh`) as root using `sudo`. The line `$FILE $PATTERN $APPIMAGE /usr $VERBOSE` runs the extracted script with elevated privileges and passes user-controllable inputs, including the AppImage path and a regex pattern. Since the script does not validate the contents of the AppImage or the extracted file, an attacker could supply a malicious AppImage that drops harmful code, leading to arbitrary code execution as root.

Furthermore, the script combines user input (via `read -p`) with filesystem operations and privilege escalation without input sanitization or source verification. The fact that it automatically extracts and runs code from an AppImage — a format designed to bundle and execute applications — means that this script effectively allows untrusted code to be silently elevated to root privileges. This behavior constitutes a supply-chain risk and could be exploited to install backdoors or exfiltrate system data.

Even if the AppImage is intended to be from a trusted upstream, the lack of checksum verification, code signing, or sandboxing during installation makes this script dangerously permissive. Such behavior falls outside standard AUR packaging practices and poses a genuine security threat.

---

## updater.sh

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Privilege escalation followed by forced directory deletion

Details:

The script `updater.sh` performs a dangerous operation by unconditionally removing the entire package directory with `rm -rf "$PACKAGE_DIR"` after attempting to run `install.sh`, regardless of whether that directory contains only temporary files or the original cloned repository. The variable `PACKAGE_DIR` is derived from the script's own location via `$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)`, meaning it points to the directory where this script resides. After the `install.sh` execution, the script changes to `/tmp` and deletes the previously determined `PACKAGE_DIR` — effectively allowing arbitrary deletion of files in the original script's location, which could include user data or other important files if symlinked or misused.

More critically, the script invokes `pkexec bash -c "$PACKAGE_DIR/install.sh"` with elevated privileges. This is equivalent to running an arbitrary script as root without any integrity checks or sandboxing. Combined with the final `rm -rf` on the script's own directory, this creates a path for privilege escalation and persistent system modification: if an attacker replaces or injects code into `install.sh` (or `updater.sh` itself), the script will execute it as root and then attempt to delete the evidence from disk — a behavior typical of malicious updater logic.

This pattern — elevating privileges to run a local script, then deleting the script's source directory — deviates significantly from standard AUR packaging practices. It introduces supply-chain risks and acts as a potential self-deleting backdoor mechanism, especially since there is no verification of the `install.sh` content before execution.

---

## patch-qt

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Modifies system libraries outside build scope

Details:

The script performs unauthorized file modifications on system libraries (`/usr/lib/libQt6Core.so.6` and `/usr/lib/libQt6Widgets.so.6`) by copying them to a different directory (`/usr/lib/eID_klient/`) and removing a section (`.note.gnu.property`) using `objcopy`. This behavior is highly suspicious and dangerous for several reasons:

First, the script operates on files outside the package build directory, directly accessing `/usr/lib/`, which violates standard packaging practices. Package builds must not modify or depend on the host system’s installed files, especially not by copying binaries from system directories during package installation or patching.

Second, manipulating binary sections of shared libraries can alter runtime behavior, potentially bypassing security mechanisms (e.g., control flow integrity checks). The removal of `.note.gnu.property`, a section used by modern glibc for feature testing and hardening, could weaken security assumptions and is not justified by typical packaging requirements. The use of `objcopy` in this context resembles binary patching often seen in supply chain attacks to disable security checks or inject backdoors.

The script provides no validation that source libraries exist, runs with no safeguards, and assumes absolute system paths, further indicating it is not intended for reproducible, safe packaging.

---

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/eidklient-native.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-2oehndi1...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-2oehndi1
Analyzing eidklient-native AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-2oehndi1) with deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD's global scope performs only variable assignments and no-op expansions (`: "${pkgname[@]}"` etc.) that do not execute code. The `source` array uses `printf` to construct archive.org URLs, which are evaluated at source time but only produce string values—no command substitution or backtick execution beyond that. There is no `pkgver()` function. The `--noprepare` and `--nobuild` flags prevent `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` from running. Therefore, fetching the sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD does not execute any malicious payload or trigger unwanted downloads/execution. All source definitions are to legitimate upstream archives with verified checksums.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code runs during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The `eidklient.install` script is a standard Arch Linux package install script. It performs routine administrative tasks:

- Removing old Qt6 libraries from `/usr/lib/eID_klient/` on removal.

LLM auditresponse for patch-qt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `patch-qt` uses `objcopy` to remove the `.note.gnu.property` section from two Qt shared libraries (`libQt6Core.so.6` and `libQt6Widgets.so.6`) and writes the modified copies to `/usr/lib/eID_klient/`. This is a standard packaging technique to patch system libraries for compatibility with the eID client application. The script does not modify the original system libraries, does not fetch any external code, and contains no obfuscation, network requests, or dangerous commands beyond the intended `objcopy` operation. The use of HTML entities (`"`) for quoting is harmless and likely an artifact of how the file was extracted. There is no evidence of malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>Legitimate library patching script, no security concerns.</summary>
</security_assessment>

data.reasoning: The unsafe findings are false positives: qt.hook/patch-qt are legitimate package operations, install.sh/updater.sh are not executed during makepkg. PKGBUILD sources are checksum-verified archives.
data.disagreement_analysis: Qwen overreacted to post-install scripts and misinterpreted patching operations. Deepseek correctly contextualized build/install boundaries.
false_positives: qt.hook: Auto-executing patch-qt is normal for this package; hook is standard
install.sh: Not executed during makepkg; merely installed for user use
updater.sh: Not executed during makepkg; user-triggered operation
patch-qt: Modifies package-owned files in /usr/lib/eID_klient, not system libraries
learnings: Improve prompt: Emphasize that only makepkg-triggered executions (build/package/prepare/check) are in-scope
Train models to recognize AUR-specific patterns: SKIP checksums, archive.org sources, Qt patching workflows
Prioritize PKGBUILD/.install reviews over helper scripts not executed during build
Reduce false alarms on post-install hooks when they call package-owned scripts
